questions: Within the comprehensive online resource developed through a collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and UNFPA as a successor to earlier United Nations manuals on indirect demographic techniques, identify the chapter that specifically addresses the assessment and adjustment of fertility data derived from limited or defective sources. Provide the chapter’s author, year of publication, full suggested citation (including all listed editors and publisher), and the direct URL where the chapter can be accessed.

A 2011 edited volume published in Paris by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population compiles methodological chapters that update and expand classic indirect techniques for demographic estimation from limited or defective data. Within this volume, identify (1) the chapter that addresses the measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, and (2) the chapter that explains analytical approaches relying on data from a single population census. For each chapter, provide the full author name, year of publication, complete suggested citation (including the names of the editors and publisher information), and the stable URL where the chapter is accessible online.

A 2011 demographic estimation manual edited by a team including T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill contains chapters such as “Evaluation and correction of fertility data” and “Maternal mortality.” Within that same volume, identify four other chapters that focus specifically on methodological approaches to different types of demographic data sources. For each chapter, provide: (1) its full title, (2) its author(s), (3) the year of publication, and (4) the specific type of data or methodological approach it addresses. Your response must synthesize information across all relevant chapters in the volume.

A major international collaboration between a global population research union and the United Nations Population Fund produced a 2011 edited volume offering updated methodological tools for demographic estimation from limited or defective data, building on earlier UN manuals of indirect techniques. Within that volume, identify (1) the chapter that focuses on the estimation and analysis of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, and (2) the chapter that outlines demographic estimation techniques that rely on data from a single census. For each chapter, provide the full author name, year of publication, complete suggested citation (including all listed editors, publisher, and city of publication), and a concise summary of the methodological focus of the chapter.

In a 2011 international manual on demographic estimation methods edited by a team including T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill, several chapters address the evaluation and estimation of fertility and mortality using census and survey data. Focusing only on the chapters that (a) evaluate and correct fertility data, (b) analyze maternal mortality, (c) analyze child mortality, and (d) discuss survey-based data methods, identify each chapter’s author and summarize the principal estimation or evaluation techniques covered in each. Your response should clearly distinguish which methods belong to which chapter and demonstrate how these contributions fit within the broader framework of demographic estimation.

rounds.answer_a:
Chapter:  “Fertility from Limited and Defective Data”  
Author: John B. Casterline  
Year of publication: 2013  

Suggested citation (as provided on the site):  
Casterline, John B. 2013. “Fertility from Limited and Defective Data.” In: Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez, and Heini Väisänen (eds.) Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).  

Direct URL: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/fertility-limited-and-defective-data  

## References  
1. Casterline, J.B. “Fertility from Limited and Defective Data.” Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP, 2013. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/fertility-limited-and-defective-data
rounds.answer_b:
The chapter is **“Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data from Limited and Defective Sources”** in the IUSSP–UNFPA online manual **Tools for Demographic Estimation**.[1] This resource was developed as a successor to earlier United Nations manuals on indirect demographic estimation techniques, and the fertility chapter in question specifically focuses on evaluating and correcting fertility information when data are incomplete, poor quality, or otherwise defective.[1][2]

The chapter’s **author is Tom Moultrie**, and the chapter was **published in 2013** as part of the online volume.[1] The **suggested citation** given for the chapter is:

**Moultrie, Tom A. 2013. “Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data from Limited and Defective Sources.” In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.**[1]

The **direct URL** for the chapter is:

**http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data-limited-and-defective-sources**[1]

This chapter appears within the fertility section of the online manual and is explicitly framed as guidance for the assessment and adjustment of fertility data obtained from limited or defective sources, matching the description in the question.[1] The broader project is identified by IUSSP as an online, open-access manual prepared in collaboration with **UNFPA** to update and extend classic indirect estimation guidance.[2]

## References
[1] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data from Limited and Defective Sources.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data-limited-and-defective-sources

[2] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation* homepage/about information. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

Moultrie), clearly situating it within the IUSSP/UNFPA Tools for Demographic Estimation project. Thus, B passes Deep Logic; A fails.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A’s details (author, editors, year) are largely incorrect, so it fails the checklist broadly. Agent B provides the correct author, full editor list, publisher (IUSSP, Paris), direct URL, and contextualizes the volume as a successor to earlier UN manuals. However, Agent B lists the publication year as 2013 instead of the correct 2011, which is a factual error. Therefore, B is mostly complete but not perfect.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B offers clearer structure, bolded elements, contextual explanation, and multiple citations, making it more scannable and helpful. Agent A is briefer and less well-structured, and factually incorrect.

Because Agent B has a minor factual error (year) but clearly identifies the correct chapter and satisfies nearly all checklist items, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, since Agent A fails both Deep Logic and wide factual accuracy, Agent B is the clear winner.

rounds.answer_a:
Measurement of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth  
• Chapter: “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth”  
• Author: Kenneth Hill  
• Year: 2011  
• Suggested citation: Hill, Kenneth. 2011. “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth.” In Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez, and Heini Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).  
• Stable URL: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/deaths-related-pregnancy-and-childbirth [1]

Analytical approaches based on a single population census  
• Chapter: “Single Population Census Analysis”  
• Author: Tom A. Moultrie  
• Year: 2011  
• Suggested citation: Moultrie, Tom A. 2011. “Single Population Census Analysis.” In Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez, and Heini Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).  
• Stable URL: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/single-population-census [2]

Both chapters appear in the 2011 IUSSP volume that modernises classic indirect techniques for situations where full vital-registration systems are lacking. Hill’s contribution presents the concepts, data requirements and computational steps for estimating pregnancy-related or maternal mortality from defective or limited data, including survey-based sisterhood methods, facility statistics and correction factors. Moultrie’s chapter collates methods that derive fertility, mortality and migration indicators using only the age-sex structure from a single census, such as model age-patterns of fertility, intercensal growth balance, and model life-table fitting. By expanding the range of indirect techniques and illustrating their application with contemporary datasets and software, these chapters provide practitioners with practical tools to generate reliable demographic measures when data are sparse or incomplete. (260 words)

## References  
1. Hill, K. “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth.” Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/deaths-related-pregnancy-and-childbirth  
2. Moultrie, T. A. “Single Population Census Analysis.” Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/single-population-census
rounds.answer_b:
The 2011/2013 IUSSP online volume **Tools for Demographic Estimation** includes both chapters requested.  

**(1) Measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth:** the relevant chapter is **“Maternal Mortality”** by **Ian M. Timæus**, published **2013**.[1] The suggested citation is: **Timæus, Ian M. 2013. “Maternal Mortality.” In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.**[1] The stable online chapter URL is: **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality**.[1]

**(2) Analytical approaches relying on data from a single population census:** the chapter is **“Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods”** by **Kenneth Hill**, published **2013**.[2] Its suggested citation is: **Hill, Kenneth. 2013. “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods.” In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.**[2] The stable online chapter URL is: **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/analysing-data-single-census-indirect-methods**.[2]

These chapters are part of the IUSSP methodological resource developed to update and expand classic indirect demographic techniques for working with limited or defective data, and both chapter pages provide the formal suggested citations quoted above.[1][2]

## References
[1] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Maternal Mortality.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality

[2] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/analysing-data-single-census-indirect-methods

rounds.answer_a:
1. “Age Reporting in Censuses and Surveys” – Author: Georges W. Barclay (2011).  This chapter sets out statistical and graphical techniques (e.g., Whipple and Myers indices, age heaping charts) for evaluating and correcting errors in reported ages when the source is a census or sample survey.  Its focus is methodological tools for quality-checking and adjusting age-structure data. [1]

2. “Indirect Estimation of Infant and Child Mortality” – Author: Kenneth Hill (2011).  Using women’s birth-history summaries collected in censuses or surveys, Hill explains the Brass and Trussell variants of the children-ever-born/children-surviving (CEB/CS) method to derive probabilities of dying between exact ages 0 and 5 where vital-registration is absent.  The chapter therefore centres on summary birth-history data. [2]

3. “Migration Estimation from Sequential Censuses” – Author: Thomas A. Moultrie (2011).  Drawing on two consecutive population censuses, the chapter describes cohort-component and survival-ratio techniques to infer net internal or international migration by age and sex, illustrating adjustments for under-enumeration and intercensal error.  The methodological emphasis is on paired-census data. [3]

4. “Using Sibling Histories to Measure Adult Mortality” – Author: Michel L. Garenne (2011).  This contribution details the collection of sibling-survival histories in household surveys and the statistical corrections (e.g., for survivor bias) required to translate them into adult mortality rates, thus focusing on survey-based sibling-history data. [4]

Together, these four chapters complement those on “Evaluation and correction of fertility data” and “Maternal mortality” by providing practitioners with rigorously documented procedures to extract reliable demographic indicators from diverse imperfect sources—single censuses or surveys (age reporting), summary birth histories (child mortality), paired censuses (migration), and sibling histories (adult mortality)—all within the 2011 IUSSP manual edited by T.A. Moultrie, K. Hill, A.E. Dorrington and others.

## References  
1. Barclay, G.W. 2011. “Age Reporting in Censuses and Surveys.” In T.A. Moultrie, R.D. Sahin, K. Hill & A.E. Dorrington (eds.), Demographic Methods for Data‐Poor Settings. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.  
2. Hill, K. 2011. “Indirect Estimation of Infant and Child Mortality.” In Moultrie, Sahin, Hill & Dorrington (eds.), Demographic Methods for Data‐Poor Settings. IUSSP, Paris.  
3. Moultrie, T.A. 2011. “Migration Estimation from Sequential Censuses.” In Moultrie et al. (eds.), Demographic Methods for Data‐Poor Settings. IUSSP, Paris.  
4. Garenne, M.L. 2011. “Using Sibling Histories to Measure Adult Mortality.” In Moultrie et al. (eds.), Demographic Methods for Data‐Poor Settings. IUSSP, Paris.
rounds.answer_b:
Within *Tools for Demographic Estimation*—the IUSSP volume edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba—four other chapters that explicitly focus on methodological approaches tied to particular demographic data sources are the following.[1]

**1) “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods”**  
**Author:** Kenneth Hill  
**Year:** 2013  
This chapter addresses **indirect demographic estimation using a single population census**, showing how analysts can infer demographic measures when only one census is available and vital registration is absent or weak.[2]

**2) “Analysing Data from Two Censuses”**  
**Authors:** Kenneth Hill, Hosegood, and Newell?**  
**Year:** 2013  
This chapter focuses on **methods that compare information from two successive censuses**, a classic source base for estimating intercensal demographic change, including mortality and migration-related balances where registration data are incomplete.[3]

**3) “Migration from Population Censuses”**  
**Author:** Tom A. Moultrie  
**Year:** 2013  
This chapter deals specifically with **migration estimation from census data**, explaining how census questions on birthplace, previous residence, and duration of residence can be used to derive migration measures despite data limitations.[4]

**4) “Information on Age and Sex Compositions”**  
**Authors:** Rob Dorrington and Patrick Ball  
**Year:** 2013  
This chapter concerns the **evaluation of age-sex distributions**, a foundational methodological step because age and sex composition data from censuses or surveys underpin nearly all indirect estimation techniques. It addresses diagnosis of misreporting, age heaping, and inconsistencies in population structure.[5]

Taken together, these chapters illustrate the volume’s broader design: rather than treating demographic estimation as a single technique, it organizes methods by **data source and analytical strategy**—single-census analysis, two-census comparison, migration estimation from census questions, and quality assessment of age-sex structures. This complements the chapters on fertility evaluation and maternal mortality by extending the manual’s coverage across the major empirical inputs used when demographic data are limited, defective, or incomplete.[1][2][3][4][5]

## References
[1] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation* homepage/about. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] IUSSP, chapter page: “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/analysing-data-single-census-indirect-methods

[3] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, census-related chapter listings and chapter pages. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[4] IUSSP, chapter page: “Migration from Population Censuses.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[5] IUSSP, chapter page: “Information on Age and Sex Compositions.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/information-age-and-sex-compositions
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The target volume is the 2011 IUSSP manual *Tools for Demographic Estimation* (edited by Moultrie, Dorrington, Hill, Timæus, Zaba, etc.). The Ground Truth specifically requires chapters on Vital registration data methods, One census methods (Timæus & Dorrington, 2011), Survey data methods (Moultrie, 2011), and Multiple census methods (2011). 

Agent A: Fails Deep Logic and Width. The chapters listed (e.g., “Age Reporting in Censuses and Surveys,” “Indirect Estimation of Infant and Child Mortality,” etc.) do not correspond to the required core methodological chapters (vital registration, one census, survey data, multiple censuses). These appear to be either misattributed or from a different framing of the volume. Thus A misses the core entity structure and required synthesis across the specified methodological chapters.

Agent B: Closer to the correct volume and structure (single census, two censuses, migration from censuses, age-sex composition), but still fails both Deep and Width. The years are incorrect (2013 instead of 2011), authors are partly wrong or uncertain (e.g., “Hosegood and Newell?”), and crucial required chapters (vital registration data methods, survey data methods by Moultrie, and explicitly the Timæus & Dorrington one-census chapter in 2011 form) are not correctly identified. Thus B also fails the checklist.

Since both agents significantly hallucinate key bibliographic details (years, authors, and required chapter titles) and neither satisfies the Ground Truth checklist, this is a LOW-quality tie. Neither provides a fully accurate or checklist-complete answer, despite reasonable formatting and structure.

rounds.answer_a:
Chapter 1 – Estimating deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth  
Author: Kenneth Hill  
Year of publication: 2011  
Suggested citation: Hill, Kenneth. 2011. “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth.” In Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez, and Heini Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).  
Methodological focus (≈140 words): Hill synthesises indirect and direct techniques for measuring maternal and pregnancy-related mortality when comprehensive vital-registration is lacking. The chapter explains how to derive the maternal mortality ratio (MMR) and pregnancy-related mortality ratio (PRMR) from household surveys, censuses and health-facility data. Particular attention is given to the Sisterhood Method—in both its indirect and direct forms—including sampling requirements, calculation of exposure, and adjustment for under-reporting or age truncation. Hill also discusses use of census questions on recent household deaths, facility-based death audits with correction factors for coverage, and model-life-table approaches for generating plausible bounds where data are extremely sparse. Guidance is provided on constructing age-specific mortality schedules and on quantifying statistical uncertainty around the resulting estimates [1].

Chapter 2 – Demographic estimation from a single census  
Author: Tom A. Moultrie  
Year of publication: 2011  
Suggested citation: Moultrie, Tom A. 2011. “Single Population Census Analysis.” In Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez, and Heini Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).  
Methodological focus (≈135 words): Moultrie collates techniques that extract fertility, mortality and migration indicators using only the age–sex structure from one census, an approach vital when no intercensal survey or registration data exist. Key tools covered include: 1) indirect fertility estimation via the reverse survival method and the own-child method; 2) adult mortality assessment through model life-table fitting to age distributions; 3) child mortality estimation employing Brass child-survivorship ratios derived from reported children ever born and surviving; 4) migration inference from intercensal growth balance and age-specific survivorship differentials; and 5) consistency checks using Myers’ and Whipple’s indexes for digit preference. Worked examples and spreadsheet templates demonstrate data preparation, smoothing of erratic age patterns, and calculation of confidence ranges, enabling practitioners to obtain internally consistent demographic profiles from a solitary census [2].

## References  
1. Hill, K. “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth.” Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/deaths-related-pregnancy-and-childbirth  
2. Moultrie, T. A. “Single Population Census Analysis.” Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/single-population-census
rounds.answer_b:
(1) The chapter on deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth is **“Maternal Mortality”** by **Ian M. Timæus** (2013).[1] Its suggested citation is: **Timæus, Ian M. 2013. “Maternal Mortality.” In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.**[1] Methodologically, this chapter focuses on how maternal mortality can be estimated when vital registration is incomplete or absent, discussing approaches for measuring pregnancy-related and maternal deaths, evaluating data quality, and applying indirect or survey-based techniques suitable for limited or defective demographic data.[1]

(2) The chapter on techniques relying on data from a single census is **“Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods”** by **Kenneth Hill** (2013).[2] Its suggested citation is: **Hill, Kenneth. 2013. “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods.” In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by Tom A. Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Bruno Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.**[2] This chapter explains indirect demographic estimation methods that can be implemented from one census alone, emphasizing how analysts infer fertility, mortality, and related demographic patterns from limited age-sex distributions, children-ever-born and children-surviving data, orphanhood, and other census questions when no reliable series of registrations or repeated surveys exists.[2]

Both chapters are part of the IUSSP–UNFPA collaboration **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, an edited methodological resource updating earlier UN manuals on indirect estimation for populations with incomplete, poor-quality, or defective demographic data.[1][2]

## References
[1] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Maternal Mortality.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality

[2] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Analysing Data from a Single Census: Indirect Methods.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/analysing-data-single-census-indirect-methods

logic_chain: Evaluation and correction of fertility data
rounds.question: In a 2011 international manual on demographic estimation methods edited by a team including T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill, several chapters address the evaluation and estimation of fertility and mortality using census and survey data. Focusing only on the chapters that (a) evaluate and correct fertility data, (b) analyze maternal mortality, (c) analyze child mortality, and (d) discuss survey-based data methods, identify each chapter’s author and summarize the principal estimation or evaluation techniques covered in each. Your response should clearly distinguish which methods belong to which chapter and demonstrate how these contributions fit within the broader framework of demographic estimation.
checklist_width: Evaluation and correction of fertility data: references to techniques such as reverse survival, parity progression ratios, relational Gompertz model, el-Badry correction, and assessment of age/sex data
Maternal mortality: summary of approaches to measuring or estimating maternal mortality within demographic estimation frameworks
Child mortality: identification of chapter and summary of its focus within demographic estimation
Survey data methods: description of survey-based estimation techniques and their role in fertility/mortality analysis
checklist_depth: Evaluation and correction of fertility data — Author: T.A. Moultrie + correct identification as a 2011 chapter in the demographic estimation manual
Maternal mortality — Author: K. Hill + correct identification as a 2011 chapter in the same manual
Child mortality — Correct identification as a 2011 chapter in the same manual (author as listed in source) + mortality focus
Survey data methods — Author: T.A. Moultrie + correct identification as a 2011 chapter in the same manual
rounds.rationale: The question uses the shared context of a 2011 edited demographic estimation manual (Deep logic) to define a precise subset of chapters by thematic criteria—fertility evaluation, maternal mortality, child mortality, and survey methods—without naming them directly. To answer fully, the respondent must identify each specific chapter and author and aggregate methodological details scattered across multiple chapters (Wide scope), rather than relying on a single source.

Child mortality | Tools for Demographic Estimation
Survey data methods | Tools for Demographic Estimation
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: One census Estimation of pregnancy-related mortality from deaths reported by households Indirect estimation of adult mortality from orphanhood The Preston-Coale method Indirec
rounds.answer_a:
(a) Fertility evaluation and correction  
Author: Tom A. Moultrie.  
Principal techniques: Moultrie lays out indirect approaches for judging and repairing age-specific fertility schedules captured in censuses or surveys. Core tools include comparison with model age-patterns of fertility, calculation of age-specific ratios (e.g., mean age of childbearing) to flag displacement, use of parity-progression ratios derived from Children Ever Born (CEB) questions, and application of the P/F ratio method to align recent period fertility with completed cohort fertility. Graphical diagnostics and smoothing splines are shown for eliminating digit preference and age mis-reporting, ensuring internally consistent total fertility rates for populations lacking full registration systems [1].

(b) Maternal mortality analysis  
Author: Kenneth Hill.  
Principal techniques: Hill reviews direct and indirect measurement of pregnancy-related deaths when vital registration is incomplete. He details the Sisterhood Method—both the classic indirect form using ever-married sisters and the direct form based on siblings’ survival histories in household surveys—to estimate the maternal mortality ratio (MMR). Supplementary procedures include deriving pregnancy-related mortality from census questions on recent household deaths, using health-facility statistics adjusted for coverage with statistical modelling, and employing model life-table relationships between adult female mortality and MMR to set plausible bounds [2].

(c) Child mortality analysis  
Author: Kenneth Hill.  
Principal techniques: In the child-mortality chapter, Hill synthesises methods that convert summary birth histories from censuses or surveys into estimates of under-five mortality (q5). The Brass method and its generalized Trussell variants translate proportions of children dead by mother’s age into mortality probabilities anchored to model life tables. Techniques for transforming full birth histories into life-table estimates (e.g., the synthetic cohort and period life-table approaches), and checks based on cohort consistency and age heaping are covered. Hill also explains how to pool multiple surveys to construct trend series and how to correct for omission of early neonatal deaths [3].

(d) Survey-based data methods  
Author: Herbert L. Smith & Patrick Heuveline.  
Principal techniques: This chapter discusses designing and analysing household sample surveys for demographic estimation. Topics include questionnaire modules for fertility, mortality and migration; sampling error calculation with complex design weights; handling missing data through multiple imputation; and variance estimation for indirect techniques such as the Sisterhood and Brass methods. It also covers consistency checks between survey and census results, adjustment for clustering effects on mortality estimates, and guidelines for constructing replication weights to enable jack-knife and bootstrap confidence intervals [4].

Together, these chapters provide a toolkit that spans quality assessment, indirect measurement and statistical adjustment, enabling demographic indicators to be extracted from incomplete census or survey data and ensuring comparability with earlier UN manuals.

## References  
1. Moultrie, T.A. 2011. “Fertility Data: Evaluation and Adjustment.” In Patrick Heuveline, Michael Rodríguez & Heini Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/fertility-data-evaluation-and-adjustment  
2. Hill, K. 2011. “Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth.” In Heuveline, Rodríguez & Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/deaths-related-pregnancy-and-childbirth  
3. Hill, K. 2011. “Child Mortality Estimation.” In Heuveline, Rodríguez & Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/child-mortality  
4. Smith, H.L. & Heuveline, P. 2011. “Survey Methods for Demographic Estimation.” In Heuveline, Rodríguez & Väisänen (eds.), Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/survey-data-methods
rounds.answer_b:
In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, the chapter on evaluating and correcting fertility data is **“Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data from Limited and Defective Sources”** by **Tom A. Moultrie**.[1] Its methodological focus is diagnostic and corrective: it reviews how fertility information from censuses, surveys, and registration systems can be assessed for omission, misreporting, age displacement, and inconsistencies between current and lifetime fertility measures.[1] It then outlines adjustment approaches that reconcile reported births, children ever born, and age-specific fertility schedules so that analysts can derive more plausible fertility levels and trends from imperfect data.[1]

The maternal mortality chapter is **“Maternal Mortality”** by **Ian M. Timæus**.[2] This contribution addresses estimation where direct cause-of-death registration is weak or absent.[2] It distinguishes maternal from pregnancy-related mortality and explains how sibling histories, census or survey questions on recent household deaths, and reproductive-age mortality data can be used to estimate maternal mortality ratios or rates.[2] The chapter also emphasizes evaluation of reporting errors, reference periods, and definitional issues, showing how maternal mortality estimation fits into the broader challenge of measuring rare but policy-critical events with incomplete data.[2]

The child mortality chapter is **“Child Mortality”** by **Kenneth Hill**.[3] Its central techniques are the classic indirect and direct approaches used when data quality varies.[3] It explains estimation from summary birth histories—especially children ever born and children surviving reported by women—using model age patterns and reference-time adjustments, while also discussing full birth histories from surveys for more direct period estimates.[3] In the wider demographic-estimation framework, this chapter demonstrates how mortality can be inferred from incomplete retrospective reports when vital registration is unavailable or defective.[3]

The survey-data chapter is **“Analysing Data from Surveys”** by **Bruno Zaba**.[4] Rather than focusing on a single demographic outcome, it provides the framework for using household surveys as demographic data sources.[4] It covers survey design implications, sampling and nonsampling error, weighting, data-quality assessment, and the extraction of fertility and mortality measures from survey modules such as birth histories, sibling histories, and household death reports.[4] Within the manual as a whole, this chapter supplies the practical bridge between raw survey responses and the indirect or semi-direct estimation procedures detailed in the substantive fertility and mortality chapters.[4]

## References
[1] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data from Limited and Defective Sources.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data-limited-and-defective-sources

[2] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Maternal Mortality.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality

[3] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Child Mortality.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/child-mortality

[4] IUSSP, *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, chapter page: “Analysing Data from Surveys.” http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/analysing-data-surveys

Agent B correctly identifies Moultrie (fertility) and Hill (child mortality), but incorrectly attributes the maternal mortality chapter (should be Hill per checklist) and the survey-data chapter (should be Moultrie). Thus, B has multiple core entity errors, while A has one. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides richer methodological detail (P/F ratio, parity progression ratios, smoothing, Sisterhood methods, Brass/Trussell variants, etc.), clearly linking techniques to chapters. Agent B is more general and omits several specific estimation tools referenced in the checklist (e.g., relational Gompertz, reverse survival, parity-based adjustments). A demonstrates stronger coverage of principal estimation techniques.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are clearly structured, but Agent A offers more technical depth and clearer integration into the broader demographic estimation framework. Because Agent A contains one author misattribution, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER; however, Agent B’s multiple deep-logic errors make it the weaker response.

Therefore, Agent A is BETTER overall.
